title: Chavica parvifolia
taxonomy: Chavica parvifolia is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. This family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the coffee or madder family. The Rubiaceae family is native to tropical and subtropical regions around the world, with over 600 genera and 13,000 species. Chavica parvifolia, like other members of this family, is characterized by its distinctive flowers and foliage. Further classification of Chavica parvifolia includes its placement in the genus Psychotria, although some sources may list it as a distinct genus.
uses: Chavica parvifolia, also known as the small-leaved Chavica, has various medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant's leaves are used to treat fever, headaches, and other ailments. The plant's leaves and bark contain compounds with antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which contribute to its medicinal uses. In folk medicine, Chavica parvifolia is used to treat digestive issues and skin problems. However, more research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal applications and potential benefits.

properties: Chavica parvifolia, also known as the dwarf chinchona or Mexican chinchona, is a plant species that has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties. intro: Chavica parvifolia, also known as Chavica, is a plant species belonging to the family Rubiaceae, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The health benefits of Chavica include its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, which have been traditionally used to treat various ailments such as fever, respiratory issues, and digestive problems. The bioactive constituents of Chavica include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Chavica is commonly prepared as a tea or tincture in traditional herbal medicine, as well as being incorporated into infusions and supplements to leverage its health benefits.
